beginner.question: What is responsive web design?
beginner.answer: Responsive web design (RWD) is an approach where a website adapts its layout and content to look good on all screen sizes, from mobile phones to large desktop monitors. It uses fluid grids, flexible images, and CSS media queries to create a seamless experience across devices.

beginner.question: What is the viewport meta tag and why is it needed?
beginner.answer: <meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0'> tells mobile browsers to set the viewport width to the device width and disable default zoom. Without it, mobile browsers may display a desktop-width page zoomed out, making content unreadable.

beginner.question: What is the difference between responsive and adaptive design?
beginner.answer: Responsive design uses fluid layouts that continuously adapt to any screen size. Adaptive design uses fixed layouts at specific breakpoints (e.g., separate designs for mobile, tablet, desktop). Responsive is more flexible; adaptive gives more control per breakpoint.

beginner.question: What is a media query?
beginner.answer: A media query is a CSS feature that applies styles conditionally based on device characteristics like screen width, height, orientation, or resolution. Example: @media (min-width: 768px) { ... } applies styles only when the viewport is at least 768px wide.

beginner.question: How do you make images responsive?
beginner.answer: Images are made responsive by setting max-width: 100% and height: auto in CSS. For performance, use the srcset attribute to serve different image sizes based on screen width, and the loading='lazy' attribute for offscreen images.

beginner.question: What is the mobile-first design philosophy?
beginner.answer: Mobile-first means designing for the smallest screen first, then using min-width media queries to enhance the layout for larger screens. This ensures a solid mobile experience and avoids writing 'undo' styles for desktop.

beginner.question: What are relative units in CSS?
beginner.answer: Relative units scale based on other properties: % (percentage of parent), em (relative to parent font-size), rem (relative to root font-size), vw (1% of viewport width), vh (1% of viewport height). They are essential for responsive design.

beginner.question: What is a breakpoint?
beginner.answer: A breakpoint is a specific viewport width where a website's layout changes to accommodate different screen sizes. Common breakpoints: 480px (phones), 768px (tablets), 1024px (small desktops), 1200px (large desktops).

beginner.question: How do you hide navigation on mobile and show it on desktop?
beginner.answer: Use CSS: display: none on mobile for the nav list, show a hamburger button. On desktop (media query min-width: 768px), set display: flex on the nav list and hide the hamburger. JavaScript handles toggling the mobile menu visibility.

beginner.question: What is the purpose of the loading='lazy' attribute?
beginner.answer: loading='lazy' tells the browser to defer loading off-screen images and iframes until the user scrolls near them. This reduces initial page load time and saves bandwidth, especially on mobile connections.

intermediate.question: How do you implement a mobile-first responsive grid without media queries?
intermediate.answer: Use CSS Grid with auto-fit and minmax: grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr)). This automatically creates as many columns as fit, with each column at least 250px wide, without any media queries.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between srcset and picture element?
intermediate.answer: srcset serves the same image at different resolutions/sizes for the browser to choose based on screen width and pixel density. The <picture> element allows art direction - serving different image crops or entirely different images at different breakpoints using <source> elements.

intermediate.question: How do you test responsive designs across multiple devices?
intermediate.answer: Use Chrome DevTools device mode (toggle device toolbar), test on real devices (phones, tablets), use cloud testing services (BrowserStack, Sauce Labs), use responsive design test tools (Responsinator, am I responsive), and check all breakpoints in manual testing.

intermediate.question: How do you create a responsive table that doesn't overflow on mobile?
intermediate.answer: Options: 1) Wrap in overflow-x: auto container for horizontal scroll. 2) Convert table to card layout using display: block on td/tr. 3) Hide less important columns with media queries. 4) Use a responsive table pattern that stacks rows vertically.

intermediate.question: What are the key considerations for responsive typography?
intermediate.answer: Use rem units for font sizes, set base body font-size to 16px, use a modular scale for heading sizes, limit line length to 45-75 characters, ensure proper line-height (1.5 for body), and use viewport-relative units for hero text that scales dramatically.

intermediate.question: How do you implement a responsive navigation with a hamburger menu that converts to a horizontal menu?
intermediate.answer: HTML structure with nav, toggle button, and ul. CSS: mobile has display: none on ul and visible toggle. Desktop media query: display: flex on ul, display: none on toggle. JavaScript adds click event listener on toggle to add/remove an 'open' class that shows the ul with display: flex on mobile.

intermediate.question: What is the 'clamp()' CSS function and how is it used in responsive design?
intermediate.answer: clamp(MIN, PREFERRED, MAX) sets a value that scales fluidly between a minimum and maximum. Example: font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) creates text that scales with viewport but never smaller than 1rem or larger than 2rem.

intermediate.question: How do you handle responsive iframes (YouTube embeds)?
intermediate.answer: Wrap the iframe in a container with position: relative and padding-bottom: 56.25% (16:9 aspect ratio). Set the iframe to position: absolute, width: 100%, height: 100%. This maintains the aspect ratio while scaling to any container width.

intermediate.question: What is the container query (CSS @container) and how does it differ from media queries?
intermediate.answer: Container queries (@container) apply styles based on a parent container's size rather than the viewport. This allows components to be responsive independent of the page layout. Media queries respond to the viewport; container queries respond to the containing element.

intermediate.question: How do you approach responsive design for a complex dashboard with multiple data visualizations?
intermediate.answer: Use a grid with auto-fit for widget layout, make chart containers responsive (svg viewBox, canvas sizing), use relative units for all dimensions, implement collapsible panels/sidebars, stack single-column on mobile, and use horizontal scroll for data tables.

advanced.question: How do you design a responsive e-commerce product grid that handles variable-length content?
advanced.answer: Use CSS Grid with subgrid for alignment, minmax() for column sizing, and grid-auto-rows for consistent card heights. For variable content, use line-clamp for text truncation, object-fit for images, and aspect-ratio for consistent image containers. Consider using container queries for independent component responsiveness.

advanced.question: What strategies exist for handling responsive images in a Next.js/Gatsby static site?
advanced.answer: Use Next.js Image component (or Gatsby image plugin) which automatically generates multiple sizes, uses WebP, implements lazy loading, and sets proper srcset/sizes. These tools provide blur-up placeholders, optimize CLS with implicit width/height, and serve responsive images with minimal configuration.

advanced.question: How do you implement a responsive drag-and-drop interface?
advanced.answer: Touch events (touchstart, touchmove, touchend) for mobile, mouse events for desktop. Use pointer events API for unified handling. Make drop zones responsive with min-height and padding. Ensure touch targets are at least 44x44px. Consider using a library like interact.js or dnd-kit that handles cross-device responsiveness.

advanced.question: What is the performance impact of loading all CSS (including desktop-only rules) on mobile?
advanced.answer: All CSS is downloaded regardless of which rules apply (media queries don't filter downloads). This can increase page weight. Mitigations: code-split CSS by component, use critical CSS inlining, load non-critical CSS asynchronously, and use media='print' onload='this.media='all'' pattern for non-critical stylesheets.

advanced.question: How do you handle responsive internationalization (RTL languages + different content lengths)?
advanced.answer: Use logical CSS properties (margin-inline-start, padding-inline-end) that automatically flip for RTL. Set dir='auto' or dir='rtl' appropriately. Account for longer text strings in German/Russian with flexible layouts. Use min-width on buttons and min-height on cards. Test all breakpoints in RTL mode.

advanced.question: How do you implement an accessible responsive modal/dialog?
advanced.answer: Use <dialog> element or role='dialog' with proper ARIA attributes. On mobile: full-screen modal with close button at top. On desktop: centered modal with overlay. Maintain focus trap regardless of screen size. Ensure touch targets for close buttons are adequate on mobile. Use the <dialog> element's native showModal() for built-in accessibility.

advanced.question: What is the 'responsive images' approach for high-DPI (Retina) displays?
advanced.answer: Use srcset with x descriptors: srcset='photo-1x.jpg 1x, photo-2x.jpg 2x, photo-3x.jpg 3x'. The browser selects the appropriate resolution based on device pixel ratio. Combine with w descriptors and sizes for resolution + viewport switching. Use image-set() in CSS for background images.

advanced.question: How do you implement responsive data visualization (charts/graphs) that work on all screen sizes?
advanced.answer: Use SVG with viewBox for scalable vector charts, make the SVG container width: 100% and height: auto. For canvas charts, resize on window resize events using ResizeObserver. Consider D3.js with responsive patterns, Chart.js with responsive: true, or Nivo for React with responsive containers.

advanced.question: What is the role of the 'aspect-ratio' CSS property in responsive design?
advanced.answer: aspect-ratio defines an element's width-to-height ratio, preventing layout shifts (CLS) before content loads. It replaces the padding-bottom hack for responsive iframes and works natively: .video-wrapper { aspect-ratio: 16/9; }. Combined with max-width: 100%, it creates flexible media containers.

advanced.question: How do you implement a responsive design system that scales across multiple products?
advanced.answer: Use design tokens (CSS custom properties for spacing, typography, breakpoints) stored in a shared package. Define component variants for different contexts (compact, comfortable, spacious). Use container queries so components respond to their container, not just the viewport. Maintain a live component library (Storybook) with responsive stories.
